output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.7, "R1.2": 0.95, "R1.3": 0.85, "R1.4": 0.6, "R1.5": 0.4, "R1.6": 0.7, "R1.7": 0.5}}

Summary: I flag several structural problems that an editor or referee will catch immediately, then propose a rewritten draft that resolves them.

## Problems in the current draft

1. **The "three axes" and the "three requirements" are not the same triple, but the draft treats them as if they were.** The opening paragraph says TransitDRR decomposes performance over *orbital period, transit S/N, and stellar-variability class*. The next paragraph defines the metric as the product of three *requirements*: period agreement, depth agreement, and variability-flag agreement. S/N is a binning axis in para 1 but disappears from the definition; depth is in the definition but not in para 1. A reader cannot tell whether TransitDRR is a product of per-bin recovery rates along three physical axes, or a product of three pass/fail tests on each TCE. These are very different objects.

2. **The independence claim is asserted, not argued, and is almost certainly wrong.** Para 4 says "Because the three per-axis pass rates measure independent aspects of pipeline behavior, their product directly estimates the joint probability …" Depth recovery is mechanically coupled to S/N (which depends on depth and noise), and the stellar-variability flag affects detrending which affects depth. The product is therefore a lower bound on the joint pass rate only under positive dependence, and the wording oversells it. Either drop the independence justification and define TransitDRR as "the fraction of TCEs that pass all three tests" (which is the joint rate by construction, no independence needed), or keep the product form and justify it empirically.

3. **The metric defeats its own motivation in paragraph 4.** The contribution is framed as decomposed reporting that "exposes failure modes aggregate metrics hide." Two sentences later: "We therefore report TransitDRR as a single scalar in §4, omitting the per-axis breakdown for brevity." If only the scalar is reported, TransitDRR has been collapsed back into the kind of aggregate it was introduced to replace. The M-dwarf / 5 mmag finding in the final paragraph — which is the most compelling result — actually requires the per-axis breakdown, so it contradicts the "omitting for brevity" sentence.

4. **The novelty claim is too broad.** "First decomposed evaluation framework for exoplanet transit pipelines" overlooks the Kepler/TESS completeness-and-reliability literature (Burke et al. 2015; Christiansen et al. 2020; Bryson et al. 2020) that already reports recovery as a function of period and S/N, and the TLS completeness maps in Hippke & Heller (2019). The contribution is better framed as: (a) joint over three axes including stellar-variability class, and (b) reduced to a single comparable scalar plus a decomposition.

5. **The 0.80 recommendation threshold is unjustified.** Where does 0.80 come from? Calibration against a downstream science target (e.g., expected yield, false-alarm budget for extended-mission vetting) needs to be stated, or the threshold should be removed from the framing section and pushed into §4.

6. **Minor prose issues.** "of a kind not previously possible" is unfalsifiable filler; "1σ" needs the noise model specified (is σ the per-point photometric scatter, or the propagated depth uncertainty?); "0.1%" should say *fractional* period agreement; "Year-1 injection set" should specify how many injections.

## Proposed revision

> Modern exoplanet detection pipelines applied to TESS Full-Frame-Image (FFI) light curves are typically evaluated by aggregate metrics — recall at a fixed false-positive rate, or completeness averaged over an injection set — that conflate pipeline behavior across very different regions of transit parameter space. Completeness maps in period and signal-to-noise (S/N) have a long history in the Kepler pipeline literature (Burke et al. 2015; Christiansen et al. 2020), but no comparable framework exists for TESS FFI searches, where stellar variability is the dominant systematic and pipelines differ sharply in how they treat it.
>
> We introduce **TransitDRR** (Transit Decomposed Reliability Ratio), an evaluation framework in which each Threshold Crossing Event from a pipeline must pass three injection-recovery tests: (i) fractional period agreement within 10⁻³; (ii) recovered transit depth within the propagated 1σ depth uncertainty of the injected value; and (iii) agreement between the pipeline's stellar-variability flag and the FFI-derived variability class used at injection. TransitDRR is the fraction of injected transits for which all three tests pass — a joint recovery rate, equivalent by construction to the joint probability without any independence assumption. We further report the three marginal pass rates, stratified along orbital period, transit S/N, and host-star variability class, so that the scalar and its decomposition are presented together.
>
> Relative to existing TESS pipeline benchmarks, the contribution is (a) joint scoring across a stellar-variability axis that aggregate recall does not isolate, and (b) a single scalar that remains comparable across pipelines while preserving the per-axis structure that diagnoses *where* a pipeline fails.
>
> Across nine pipelines from the QLP (Huang et al. 2020), BLS (Kovács et al. 2002), and transit-least-squares (Hippke & Heller 2019) families, TransitDRR on the Year-1 injection set (N = [TK] injections) ranges from 0.34 to 0.71. The decomposition reveals a sharp drop in recovery for M-dwarf hosts with photometric variability above 5 mmag — a regime in which aggregate recall stays within 5% of its overall mean, and which a scalar benchmark would therefore miss. Calibration of a TransitDRR threshold for extended-mission use is deferred to §4.

## Uncertainty disclosure

The citations I suggested (Burke 2015, Christiansen 2020, Bryson 2020, Hippke & Heller 2019, Huang 2020, Kovács 2002) are standard references in this subfield but I have not verified author lists, years, or that each is the best citation for the specific claim — please check before insertion. The claim that the three requirements are dependent (depth ↔ S/N ↔ variability) is a physical-reasoning argument, not one I verified against your injection-recovery data; if your data show the product closely matches the joint rate, the original independence framing could be defended empirically and that should be stated.
